source.title: 50+ Types of Camera Shots, Angles, and Techniques

categories.name: Shot Size
categories.description: How much of the subject and surrounding space is framed.

categories.name: Camera Position
categories.description: Where the camera is placed vertically relative to the subject.

categories.name: Camera Angle
categories.description: The direction or tilt the camera uses in relation to the subject.

categories.name: Camera Movement
categories.description: How the camera or lens changes during the shot over time.

entries.name: Establishing Shot
entries.description: Introduces the setting and scene geography before focusing tighter on the action.

entries.name: Extreme Wide Shot
entries.description: Places the subject very small within a large environment to emphasize scale, distance, or isolation.

entries.name: Wide Shot
entries.description: Shows the full subject with plenty of surrounding space so the location stays clearly visible.

entries.name: Full Shot
entries.description: Frames the full body while still leaving some attention on the environment around the subject.

entries.name: Medium Wide Shot
entries.description: Frames the subject from roughly the knees up, balancing body language with environmental context.

entries.name: Cowboy Shot
entries.description: Frames the subject from around mid-thigh up, often keeping hands, props, or a holster area in view.

entries.name: Medium Shot
entries.description: Frames roughly from the waist up for a versatile balance of performance and surroundings.

entries.name: Medium Close-Up
entries.description: Frames from roughly the chest or shoulders up to emphasize facial performance while keeping a little context.

entries.name: Close-Up
entries.description: Frames the face or a key subject tightly to concentrate attention and emotion.

entries.name: Extreme Close-Up
entries.description: Frames a very small detail, such as eyes or an object feature, for maximum emphasis.

entries.name: Eye Level
entries.description: Places the camera at the subject's eye line for a neutral, natural-feeling perspective.

entries.name: Shoulder Level
entries.description: Places the camera around shoulder height, which often feels slightly more natural for standing subjects.

entries.name: Hip Level
entries.description: Places the camera around waist or hip height, useful when hands, props, or stride matter in the frame.

entries.name: Knee Level
entries.description: Places the camera around knee height to give subjects more scale and emphasize legs or forward movement.

entries.name: Ground Level
entries.description: Places the camera on or near the ground to emphasize the floor plane, feet, debris, or a dramatic low vantage point.

entries.name: Level Angle
entries.description: Points straight at the subject without an upward, downward, or tilted bias.

entries.name: Low Angle
entries.description: Looks up at the subject from below, often making them feel more powerful, imposing, or dominant.

entries.name: High Angle
entries.description: Looks down at the subject from above, often making them feel smaller, weaker, or exposed.

entries.name: Dutch Angle
entries.description: Tilts the camera off level so the horizon slants, creating disorientation, tension, or instability.

entries.name: Overhead Shot
entries.description: Looks straight down from high above to emphasize layout, movement patterns, or graphic composition.

entries.name: Aerial Shot
entries.description: Captures the scene from very high above, usually to establish broad geography and large-scale movement.

entries.name: Static Shot
entries.description: Keeps the camera locked off without movement so performance, blocking, or action within the frame carries the scene.

entries.name: Dolly Shot
entries.description: Moves the camera physically through space, such as pushing in, pulling back, or traveling alongside the subject.

entries.name: Zoom Shot
entries.description: Changes the lens focal length during the shot to move tighter or wider without moving the camera itself.

entries.name: Dolly Zoom
entries.description: Combines camera movement and lens zoom at the same time to create a warped change in perspective.

entries.name: Pan
entries.description: Rotates the camera side to side on a horizontal axis to reveal space or follow action.

entries.name: Tilt
entries.description: Rotates the camera up or down on a vertical axis to reveal height or follow motion.

entries.name: Whip Pan
entries.description: Pans rapidly enough to create motion blur, often for energy, transitions, or sudden reveals.

entries.name: Whip Tilt
entries.description: Tilts rapidly enough to create motion blur, often for heightened energy or abrupt transitions.

entries.name: Tracking Shot
entries.description: Moves with the subject so the viewer travels alongside, behind, or with them through the scene.

entries.name: Crab Shot
entries.description: Moves the camera horizontally beside the subject, like a lateral dolly move across the scene.

entries.name: Arc Shot
entries.description: Moves the camera around the subject in an arc to reveal space and shift perspective dynamically.
